rulings.Case Title: DEIDRENNE ASHCRAFT VS. ROE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Exclude Witness Nichole West And To Exclude Evidence Of Pltf'S New Injury Claim Developed With Assistance Of Counsel After The Close Of Discovery And After Disclosure Of Expert Witnesses, Or In The Alternative, For An Order Allowing Defts To Conduct Discovery Of This New Evidence Only (**See Notes**)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Thursday, April 17, 2014, Line 1, PLAINTIFF DEIDRENNE ASHCRAFT'S Motion To Exclude Witness Nichole West And To Exclude Evidence Of Plaintiff's New Injury Claim Developed With Assistance Of Counsel After The Close Of Discovery And After Disclosure Of Expert Witnesses, Or In The Alternative, For An Order Allowing Defendants To Conduct Discovery Of This New Evidence Only. Pro Tem Judge James Watson, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL VS. BANK OF THE WEST, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, April 17, 2014, Line 10, DEFENDANT BANK OF THE WEST's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative For Summary Adjudication. Defendant's motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant's motion for summary adjudication is granted in part and denied in part. Issues 1-11 set forth in the Notice are subsumed in the ruling on each cause of action. As to the first cause of action, summary adjudication is denied. Plaintiff has raised triable issues as to whether Boyer had discriminatory intent, whether the conduct was severe or pervasive enough to give rise to harassment, whether Defendant took immediate and appropriate corrective action, and whether the alleged discriminatory conduct was taken within the course and scope of agency or employment. As to the second cause of action, summary adjudication is granted. "[T]o establish constructive discharge, an employee must plead and prove¿that the employer either intentionally created or knowingly permitted working conditions that were so intolerable or aggravated at the time of the employee's resignation that a reasonable employer would realize that a reasonable person in the employee's position would be compelled to resign." (Turner v. Anheuser-Busch, Inc. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 1238, 1251.) The evidence does not raise a triable issue of material fact as to whether Plaintiff had "no reasonable alternative except to quit." (Id.) The undisputed facts show that Defendant informed Plaintiff prior to her resignation that Plaintiff would never work on a project with Boyer again (AMF 65), Defendant informed Plaintiff prior to her resignation that Boyer would not work for Defendant in the future (UMF 52), Plaintiff informed her friend in Florida prior to her resignation that moving there was her "dream" (UMF 36, 76), Plaintiff desired to move to Florida to "recover [from her] financial situation" and to "enjoy life" by getting rid of her mortgage so she would have "free money on hand so [she] can live [her] life" (UMF 41, 45), and Plaintiff offered to continue working for Defendant at the time of her resignation, provided that she could work remotely from Florida (AMF 71). As to the third cause of action, summary adjudication is granted as unopposed. As to the fourth cause of action, summary adjudication is granted. The FEHC has explicitly held that Gov. Code sec. 12940(k) does not "give rise to an actionable tort or private cause of action in civil proceedings between private litigants." (DFEH v. Lyddan Law Group (Cal. F.E.H.C. 2010) 2010 CAFEHC Lexis 4, *35.) Courts "ordinarily give substantial weight to the FEHC's construction of the statutes under which it operates" unless "the agency's interpretation is clearly erroneous or unauthorized." (Kelly v. Methodist Hospital of Southern Cal. (2000) 22 Cal.4th 1108, 1118.) As to the twelfth issue, summary adjudication is denied. Defendant is seeking to adjudicate that damages "will be limited." (Notice, p. 2.) This issue cannot be properly disposed of by summary adjudication. (CCP sec. 437c(f)(1).) As to the thirteenth issue, summary adjudication is granted. The undisputed facts show that Plaintiff offered to continue work, so long as she could do so remotely in Florida. (AMF 71.) As to the fourteenth issue, summary adjudication is denied. There is a triable issue as to whether Thomason was informed by other employees about Boyer's treatment of Plaintiff, but did not take immediate remedial action. (AMF 73-75.) See the tentative rulings on the two other motions in this matter for instructions on how to contest a tentative ruling and submit a proposed order. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL VS. BANK OF THE WEST, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION ; Declaration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, April 17, 2014, Line 10, PLAINTIFF NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. Denied without prejudice. Plaintiff has not met its initial burden. Plaintiff relies on Defendant's insufficient responses to its special interrogatories as the primary basis for this motion. However, on January 18, 2014, the day after Plaintiff filed this motion, Defendant served amended responses to Plaintiff's special interrogatories pursuant to an Order of this Court granting Plaintiff's motion to compel. Because the responses Plaintiff relies on have been superseded by amended responses, this motion is denied without prejudice. rulings.Case Title: NETSANET HAILEMICHAEL VS. BANK OF THE WEST, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, April 17, 2014, Line 10, DEFENDANTs SINOPA GROUP LLC and NOLEN BOYER's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Denied. In 1959, the legislature passed the Unruh Act to expand "the reach of [the prior public accommodations statute] from common carriers and places of public accommodation and recreation¿to include 'all business establishments of every kind whatsoever.'" (Marina Point, Ltd. v. Wolfson (1982) 30 Cal.3d 721, 731.) "Any business or organization, whether for-profit, or non-profit, may be deemed a business establishment if it performs customary business functions and fulfills a businesslike purpose." (O'Connor v. Village Green Owners Assn. (1983) 33 Cal.3d 790, 796.) The evidence establishes that Defendants are a business that provides project management, cost management, and development consulting to business clients. (UMF no. 2.) As such, Defendants are a "business establishment[] of every kind whatsoever" covered by the Unruh Act. (See Wilkins-Jones v. County of Alameda (N.D. Cal. 2012) 859 F.Supp.2d 1039, applying California law, holding that a for-profit, private contractor hired by a county to provide medical care assessment services for the county jail inmates was a business establishment as contemplated by the Unruh Act.) California cases have "allowed parties that are not clients, patrons, or customers in the traditional sense to bring Unruh Act claims." (Strother v. Southern California Permanente Medical Group (9th Cir. 1996) 79 F.3d 859, 873.) In Jackson v. Sup. Ct. (1994) 30 Cal.App.4th 936, 942, the court held that an investment advisor who accompanied two clients to a bank could bring an Unruh Act claim against the bank because he was "pursuing an accommodation, advantage, or privilege of the bank within the meaning of the Unruh Act when he accompanied his clients to the bank to aid them in transacting their business." The evidence establishes here that Plaintiff was aiding her employer, Bank of the West, in transacting its business with Defendants when the alleged discrimination occurred. Therefore, the conduct complained of falls under the Unruh Act. Lastly, Plaintiff was not an employee or independent contractor of Defendants and thus the Unruh Act applies. Plaintiff was not compensated for her work by Defendants. Rather, Defendants were hired and paid to provide services to Plaintiff's employer, and by virtue of that employment relationship, to Plaintiff. (See Alch v. Sup. Ct. (2004) 122 Cal.App.4th 339, 393, fn. 56.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)
